Maphumulo and Another v S (AR 226/09) [2010] ZAKZPHC 39; 2010 (2) SACR 550 (KZP) (23 July 2010)
Download original files

IN
THE KWAZULU NATAL HIGH COURT, PIETERMARITZBURG
REPUBLIC
OF SOUTH AFRICA
CASE
NO. AR 226/09
In
the matter between:
XOLANI
SIFISO DUMISANI MAPHUMULO 		     FIRST APPELLANT
MPUMELELO
MFANAFUTHI CHAMANE		SECOND APPELLANT
and
THE
STATE								  RESPONDENT
J U D G M E N T
MSIMANG JP
[1]	The first and
fifth appellants
were, respectively, accused 1 and 5 in the Court
a
quo
and each one of them was in that Court convicted of the crime of
murder and on four counts of attempted murder.  In addition, the
first appellant was convicted on two counts of contravening two
sections of the
Firearms Control Act 60 of 2000
, the first count
relating to unlawful possession of a prohibited firearm and the
second one relating to unlawful possession of
ammunition.
[2]	On the
conviction of
the crime of murder the first appellant was sentenced to serve a term
of life imprisonment and the four convictions on the counts
of
attempted murder were taken together for the purpose of sentence and
he was sentenced to serve a term of 10 years’ imprisonment
in
respect of those convictions and two convictions on the two counts of
contravening the sections of the
Firearms Control Act were
also taken
together for the purpose of sentence and he was sentenced to serve a
term of 5 years’ imprisonment in respect
of those convictions.
[3]	The fifth
appellant was sentenced to serve a term of 20 years’
imprisonment for the murder conviction and, as it was the
case in his
co-appellant, the convictions on four counts of attempted murder were
taken together for the purpose of sentence and
he was sentenced to
serve a term of 10 years’ imprisonment in respect of those
convictions.  In his case, the Court, however,
ordered that the
sentences imposed upon him would run concurrently with the result
that he would effectively serve a term of 20
years’
imprisonment.
[4]	The two
appellants are before us on appeal directed against the convictions
only, leave to that effect having been granted by
the Court
a
quo
.
[5]	It was common
cause in the Court
a
quo
that, during the morning of 31 May 2005 at approximately 07h45, the
deceased, one
Makhosawile
Mfaniyeza Ngidi
,
was walking together with one
Siphamandla
Linda Ngidi
,
one
Thembinkosi
Ngidi
,
one
Kethokuhle
Ngidi
and one
Nkosi
Sangweni
.
 They were proceeding to a local taxi rank in the KwaMambulu area of
Kranskop where
Nkosi
Sangweni
was destined to board a public transport which would transport him to
Durban.  As they were walking down the road near a school
they were
shot at and began to run in different directions.
Thembinkosi
was struck by a bullet on his lower back and on his left shoulder
blade and, as a result, sustained injuries for which he was
subsequently hospitalised at
Njunjambili
Hospital
where he remained a patient for a period of approximately one month. 
  It is common cause that the deceased died as a result of
the
injuries he had sustained during the said shooting.  A post-mortem
report which was handed in by consent of all the parties
declared
that the deceased had died of the gunshot wounds of the head, abdomen
and chest.
[6]	Three
eyewitnesses gave evidence of identification in the Court
a
quo
and all three were able to identify the first appellant as having
formed part of the group of persons who had fired shots at them
on
the occasion.  Those eyewitnesses were
Thembinkosi
Ngidi, Siphamandla Ngidi
and
Nkosikhona
Christian Sangweni.
[7]	In addition,
eyewitness
Nkosikhona
Christian Sangweni
was
able to identify the fifth appellant as being one of the persons who
had fired shots at them.
[8]	This
incident
occurred at approximately 07h45 which was during broad daylight.  It
is not disputed that both appellants were well known
to the witnesses
and that the latter were observing the appellants from distances of
between 100-200 metres.
[9]	The appellants
gave evidence in their defence, denying that they were present at the
scene at the time of the shooting and declaring
that they were
elsewhere, the first appellant’s version being that, at the
time, he was in the township of Clermont in the
Durban area and the
fifth appellant declaring that, at the time, he was in Johannesburg.
[10]	In rejecting
the appellants’ evidence, accepting the evidence adduced by the
prosecution and accordingly convicting the
appellants of the crime of
murder and on four counts of attempted murder the Court
a
quo,
inter
alia,
pronounced
itself as follows:
“Now the
circumstances under which this crime was committed are that it
happened in broad daylight.  The distances from which
the witnesses
observed these people is not long.  And it is clear from the evidence
of Nkosikhona Sangweni that he had a clear
view of the people
advancing, because they did not see him, he was hiding behind a tree.
 In fact according to him he started observing
the people right at
the onset whilst these people were still shooting from the bush.
….
When I contrast
the evidence both of the State and the accused, I find that the State
evidence is reliable and that the probability
is that the evidence
they gave is the truth.  ...
I am of the opinion that the alibis
which had been proffered by the accused are false beyond a reasonable
doubt.  I am satisfied
that the State witnesses have given cogent,
clear evidence, which proves the case against the accused beyond a
reasonable doubt.”
[11]	It is against
this finding that the appellants now appeal, submitting that the
Court had erred in finding that the identities
of the appellants had
been proved beyond a reasonable doubt.  It was further submitted, on
behalf of the appellants, that as there
admittedly existed bad blood
between the Ngidi and Maphumulo families, the Court should have found
that the witnesses bore a motive
to falsely implicate the appellants
in the commission of the crime.
Mr
Viljoen
,
who presented argument on behalf of the fifth appellant, further
submitted that eyewitness
Nkosikhona
Christian Sangweni
was
a single witness in the evidence of identification of the fifth
appellant and therefore that the Court
a
quo
should have exercised even more caution in evaluating his evidence
which, he argued, that Court had failed to do.
[12]	The issue in
this appeal is therefore one of identification.  More specifically
the issue is whether the Court
a
quo
had erred in finding that it had been proved beyond a reasonable
doubt that the appellants had committed the crimes of murder and
attempted murder as set out in the indictment.
[13]	Evidence of
identification must always be evaluated with caution and the
locus
classicus
on the
issue is
S v Mthetwa
1
where
Holmes
JA
expressed himself as
follows on the need for such caution:
“Because of the fallibility of
human observation, evidence of identification is approached by the
Courts with some caution.
 It is not enough for the identifying
witness to be honest; the reliability of his observation must also be
tested.  This depends
on various factors, such as lighting,
visibility, and eyesight; the proximity of the witness; his
opportunity for observation,
both as to time and situation; the
extent of his prior knowledge of the accused; the mobility of the
scene; corroboration; suggestibility;
the accused’s face,
voice, build, gait, and dress; the result of identification parades,
if any; and, of course, the evidence
by or on behalf of the accused. 
The list is not exhaustive.  These factors, or such of them as are
applicable in a particular
case, are not individually decisive, but
must be weighed one against the other, in the light of the totality
of the evidence, and
the probabilities; see cases such as
R
v Masemang
, 1950 (a) SA
488 (AD);
R v Dladla and
Others
,
1962 (1) SA 307
(AD) at p. 310C;
S v
Mehlape
1963 (2) SA 29
(AD).”
2
[14]	Regarding a need for the
dependability of such evidence,
Van
den Heever JA
sounded the
following warning in
Rex
v Masemang
3
:
“The positive assurance with
which an honest witness will sometimes swear to the identity of an
accused person is itself
no guarantee of the correctness of that
evidence.  One often finds that a woman who was totally unable to
point out her assailant
on an identification parade will, when the
accused is arrested on the strength of other evidence, swear
positively at the trial
that she recognises him as her assailant. 
The innate and instinctive desire that there shall be retribution may
be conducive to
this.”
4
[15]	That the Court
a
quo
was mindful of the need
to treat this type of evidence with caution and that it was alive to
the pitfalls of an honest witness
who gives a positive assurance as
to the identity of an accused person, is evident from the following
passage quoted from the judgment:
“It is trite that when the
issue of identification is in issue, the Court must be very careful. 
There is a possibility that
an honest, but mistaken, witness may
testify and say he saw a particular person when in fact he did not.”
[16]	It would, however, appear that
such an awareness is, in itself, not sufficient.  As was stated by
Botha JA
in
S v Avon Bottle Store
(Pty) Ltd and Others
5
:
“What is necessary is that the
judicial officer, who is also the trier of fact, should demonstrate
by his treatment of the
evidence of an accomplice that he has in fact
heeded the warning.”
6
[17]	This demonstration is sorely
missing in the judgment of the Court
a
quo
.  I have already
referred to the passages in the judgment whereby that Court purported
to accept the evidence of identification
adduced on behalf of the
State and, in my judgment, it is lacking in its treatment of such
evidence with a view to demonstrating
that it had heeded the need to
apply the cautionary rule.
[18]	Besides, as already pointed out,
notwithstanding the fact that both appellants were known to all three
witnesses of identification,
only one of them could identify the
fifth appellant.  It stands to reason that such a situation demanded
that evidence of identification
of each appellant be evaluated
separately in the judgment, which the Court
a
quo
failed to do.
[19]	Furthermore, it is trite that,
among the issues which should be tested in the evidence of
identification, is the opportunity
which a witness had for a correct
identification.  This is what
Holmes
JA
referred to as “the
reliability of his observation”
7
.
  From the perusal of the record it becomes clear that the
reliability of observation of each witness of identification was not
properly tested.
[20]	I have accordingly concluded that
the record shows that the reasons given by the Court
a
quo
for accepting the
evidence of identification in this matter are unsatisfactory and
therefore that this Court is at large to disregard
the findings of
that Court on identification and to come to its own conclusion on the
matter.
8
I am mindful of the advantages which a trial court has over an
appeal Court born of the opportunity by the former Court to observe
the demeanour of witnesses and the opportunity to observe that
appearance and whole personality which, over the years, had led
to a
justified reluctance by the Appeal Courts to upset the factual
findings of the trial Courts.  In the present case I have,
however,
been driven to the conclusion that, after making due allowance for
those advantages, some of the evidence of identification
adduced on
behalf of the prosecutor cannot sustain proof beyond a reasonable
doubt.
[21]	Commencing with the allegation of
bad blood between the two families, the Court
a
quo
mentioned the subject
more than once in its judgment which shows that, throughout the trial
of the matter, the Court was alive to
the issue.  However,
notwithstanding this admitted enmity, the Court found that the
witnesses had not deliberately misled the Court.
 In other words the
Court concluded that such enmity had not caused the witnesses to
exhibit “… interest or bias adverse
to the accused”.
9
As a matter of fact, that was not the argument of the appellants in
the appeal.  I can therefore find no fault with the manner
whereby
the Court
a quo
treated the evidence of bad blood in this matter.
[22]	Regarding that Court’s
demonstration that it had taken heed of the need to apply the
cautionary rule in the evidence
of identification herein, it took
into consideration the fact that the crime had been committed in
broad daylight, that the distances
from which the witnesses were
observing the accused were not long and that witness
Nkosikhona
Sangweni
had
a clear view of the advancing assailants.  What is, however, missing
in the Court’s evaluation of the evidence, which
was also not
made clear when the witnesses testified, was the length of time
during which the witnesses were observing the appellants.
 The
determination of the length of time during which the witnesses
observed the accused is essential for the purpose of testing
the
reliability of observation of these witnesses.
[23]	As against
this deficiency in the evidence of witnesses is the fact that all
three witnesses testified that the first appellant
was very well
known to them, having grown up together with him in the same area. 
This evidence was not challenged by the first
appellant and must be
regarded as being common cause.  Evidence that the first appellant
was identified under these circumstances,
in my judgment, is
sufficient to provide proof beyond a reasonable doubt.  Also, the
fact that these witnesses, who all know the
first appellant very
well, have corroborated each other on this type of evidence has
removed all danger that a wrong person could
have been identified.
[24]	The position
is, however, different regarding the evidence of identification of
the fifth appellant.  Only
Nkosikhona
Christian Sangweni
,
could identify the fifth appellant as having been present and
participated during the shooting.  He testified that when he was
walking with the others they were suddenly fired upon and his first
reaction was to flee and to hide behind a tree.  It was when
he had
assumed this position that he observed the assailants who, at the
time, were emerging into the open and moving to a gravel
road.  There
were approximately 12 or 13 of them and, among them, was the fifth
appellant.
[25]	This witness’
opportunity to reliably observe the fifth appellant was clearly not
tested.  For example, the formation
assumed by the assailants when
they emerged into the open as well as the length of time during which
he had observed them was not
clarified.  Also, there is the issue of
how this witness had got to know this appellant.  When questioned by
the State as to how
it came about that he got to know him, the
following exchange took place:
“Accused 5, do you know him? 
---  I know him, yes.
How do you know him?  ---  I know him
as a young man of that area.
Do you know where he lives?  ---  His
home is also in that area.
And how far is his home from yours? 
--- I can also estimate it to be 25 minutes walk.”
[26]	Furthermore, it must be borne in
mind, that notwithstanding the fact that this appellant was very well
known to the other two
eyewitnesses, they failed to identify him as
one of the persons who had participated during the shooting.  As a
matter of fact,
responding to this issue, witness
Siphamandla
Linda Ngidi
stated that:
“They were many people there
and I am unable to say whether accused 5, whether he was there, but
there were many of them.”
[27]	When I was considering this
witness’ evidence, I was reminded of the following passage
quoted in the judgment in
R
v Shekelele and Another
10
“Questions of identification
are always difficult.  That is why such extreme care is always
exercised in the holding of identification
parades – to prevent
the slightest hint reaching the witness of the identity of the
suspect.  An acquaintance with the history
of criminal trials reveals
that gross injustices are not infrequently done through honest but
mistaken identifications.  People
often resemble each other. 
Strangers are sometimes mistaken for old acquaintances.  In all cases
that turn on identification the
greatest care should be taken to test
the evidence.  Witnesses should be asked by what features, marks or
indications they identify
the person whom they claim to recognise. 
Questions relating to his height, build, complexion, what clothing he
was wearing and
so on should be put.  A bald statement that the
accused is the person who committed the crime is not enough.  Such a
statement
unexplored, untested and uninvestigated, leaves the door
wide open for the possibility of mistake.  Where the accused is an
ignorant
native who is unrepresented by counsel or attorney and who
is therefore unable himself to probe the evidence of identification
and where the prosecutor has not done so, the Court should undertake
this task, as otherwise grave injustice may be done.”
11
[28]	I have no doubt that the
acceptance of and reliance on this evidence, uncorroborated as it
was, would cause grave injustice.
[29]	Regarding the conviction on
contravening sections of the
Firearms Control Act, the
State tendered
evidence that the prohibited firearm and ammunition had been found by
Constable Terrence Sibonelo
Gumede
attached to the
Kranskop Police Station at a two-roomed house situate at the KwaMashu
Section called Bester.  Upon his entry into
this structure he found
that six men were sleeping in one room and that a number of women
were sleeping in another room.  When
these men were searched,
firearms were found on their persons.  In his evidence-in-chief the
Constable had testified that he had
found a ,22 revolver under the
pillow upon which the first appellant had been sleeping.  It had
seven live rounds of ammunition
in it.  The appellant had admitted
that he did not have a licence to possess the said firearm and
ammunition.
[30]	However, during cross-examination
by
Mr Viljoen
,
the Constable conceded that he could not attach the names he had
given, as persons he had found in unlawful possession of the
firearms, to any faces of the accused who stood in the dock.  It was
for this reason that first appellant’s Counsel submitted
that
the conviction of the first appellant on counts 6 and 7 had been bad.
Ms Maphalala
,
who argued the appeal for the State, conceded, fairly in my view,
that the appeal against conviction on those counts should succeed.
[31]	In the premises the following
order is made:
First
appellant’s appeal against convictions in respect of counts 6
and 7 is upheld and the convictions on those counts, as
well as the
sentences imposed therefor, is hereby set aside.
First appellant’s appeal
against convictions on the count of murder and on the four counts of
attempted murder is, however,
dismissed and those convictions and the
sentences imposed therefor are confirmed.
Fifth appellant’s appeal
against convictions on the count of murder and on four counts of
attempted murder is upheld and
all those convictions and the
sentences imposed therefor are hereby set aside.
___________________________
MSIMANG JP
___________________________ : I
agree
JAPPIE J
___________________________  :I
agree
SWAIN J
Appeal heard on :			   14 July 2010
Counsel for the first appellant :	 
 AR Khan
Counsel for the fifth appellant :	 
 CJ Viljoen
Instructed by  the Legal Aid
Board
Counsel for the State :		   Ms NG
Maphalala
Director of Public
Prosecutions
Judgment handed down on :	   23
July 2010
